vlog.Problematic: This project focused on building a compact wireless telemetry system composed of a transmitter and a receiver for real-time motion and location tracking.[IMAGE]./aerodata-photos/aerodata-1.jpeg[/IMAGE][IMAGE]./aerodata-photos/aerodata-2.jpeg[/IMAGE][CAPTION]Figure 1: Receiver[/CAPTION][CAPTION]Figure 2: Transmitter[/CAPTION] Most available telemetry and flight-data solutions are too expensive, too large, and too power-hungry for students and UAVs. The engineering need was a low-cost, low-mass, reproducible system capable of acquiring multi-sensor data, processing it onboard, and transmitting it reliably over RF while respecting strict payload and size constraints. Within this system, I was specifically responsible for the transmitter side, including its firmware and PCB design.
vlog.Features: The system integrates orientation, altitude, and positioning sensors, performs embedded preprocessing, and streams structured telemetry packets wirelessly to a receiver with live display and dashboard support.[IMAGE]./aerodata-photos/aerodata-3.png[/IMAGE][CAPTION]Figure 3: System Architecture[/CAPTION] It uses real-time sampling, packetized RF transmission, onboard filtering, and plug-and-play operation. Both transmitter and receiver are implemented on dedicated PCBs to improve electrical reliability and mechanical robustness.[IMAGE]./aerodata-photos/aerodata-4.png[/IMAGE][CAPTION]Figure 4: live telemetry dashboard[/CAPTION] My contribution covered the complete transmitter firmware stack and the full transmitter PCB design flow from schematic to routed layout, ready for fabrication.[IMAGE]./aerodata-photos/aerodata-5.png[/IMAGE][CAPTION]Figure 5: Transmitter Schematics[/CAPTION]
vlog.Challenges: Sensor outputs were noisy and biased, requiring calibration and lightweight filtering under limited MCU resources. RF transmission required deterministic timing and strict packet formatting to prevent corruption and dropouts.[IMAGE]./aerodata-photos/aerodata-6.png[/IMAGE][CAPTION]Figure 6: Development Setup[/CAPTION] On the hardware side, integrating the MCU, RF module, sensors, and regulators on a small board created routing congestion, grounding constraints, and noise-coupling risks. Development-board footprints increased in size and restricted placement flexibility.[IMAGE]./aerodata-photos/aerodata-7.png[/IMAGE][CAPTION]Figure 7: Protoboard[/CAPTION] Firmware architecture and PCB routing were interdependent, so peripheral selection and pin mapping required multiple coordinated revisions.
vlog.Solution: A resource-constrained embedded telemetry transmitter was implemented using an Arduino Nano-class MCU, IMU, barometric sensor, GPS, and 2.4 GHz RF transceiver with timed acquisition loops, structured payload framing, complementary-style sensor fusion, and SPI/I²C peripheral control.[IMAGE]./aerodata-photos/aerodata-8.jpeg[/IMAGE][CAPTION]Figure 8: Final Transmitter PCB[/CAPTION] I developed the transmitter firmware for sensor polling, normalization, packet assembly, and deterministic RF transmission, and designed a dedicated transmitter PCB with controlled power distribution, decoupling, and layout segregation between RF, digital, and sensor domains.
vlog.LearningOutcomes: I gained hands-on experience in embedded firmware architecture, real-time sampling loops, RF packetization, and sensor-interface programming. I strengthened my PCB design skills in component placement, grounding strategy, decoupling, routing under constraints, and design-for-debug practices. The project reinforced hardware–software co-design methodology, measurement-driven iteration, and transmitter-side protocol engineering.
vlog.WhatToDoDifferently: I would move earlier to a fully custom integrated board instead of starting with stacked modules, and I would include test points, calibration hooks, and debug interfaces in the first PCB revision. I would also reserve more compute and memory margin at the architecture stage to support advanced filtering and protocol features without late optimization pressure.
vlog.FutureWorkAndWhy: Future work targets a redesign of a second-generation transmitter to increase performance, accuracy, and deployment range. The Arduino Nano-class MCU will be replaced by an STM32 Blue Pill–class microcontroller to provide higher computational capability, better timers, and stronger peripheral support for sensor fusion and communication tasks.[IMAGE]./aerodata-photos/aerodata-9.jpg[/IMAGE][CAPTION]Figure 9: STM32 Blue Pill[/CAPTION] The transmitter PCB will be redesigned as a more compact integrated board to reduce size and weight, improving UAV payload compatibility and vibration tolerance. The MPU6050-class IMU will be replaced by an MPU9050-class device to obtain lower noise, more sensing capability, and better bias stability for improved estimation quality.[IMAGE]./aerodata-photos/aerodata-10.jpg[/IMAGE][CAPTION]Figure 10: MPU-9250 Module[/CAPTION] The NRF RF module will be replaced by a LoRa transceiver to enable long-distance, low-bitrate telemetry with a higher link budget and extended operational range in field and aerial scenarios.[IMAGE]./aerodata-photos/aerodata-11.jpg[/IMAGE][CAPTION]Figure 11: LoRa SX1278 Module[/CAPTION]
